title: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual
authors: Baligh R. Yehia, Todd P. Levin

items.subtopic: Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer or Questioning, Intersex, Asexual, and More (LGBTQIA+) Health | UNDERSTANDING LGBTQIA+ HEALTH DISPARITIES
items.title: Preventive Screening Disparities in Lesbian and Bisexual Women
items.content: Lesbian and bisexual women are less likely to receive recommended preventive screenings such as breast, cervical, and colorectal cancer screenings.
keyPoints: Lesbian and bisexual women have lower rates of receiving preventive screenings compared to heterosexual women.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer or Questioning, Intersex, Asexual, and More (LGBTQIA+) Health | UNDERSTANDING LGBTQIA+ HEALTH DISPARITIES

items.subtopic: Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer or Questioning, Intersex, Asexual, and More (LGBTQIA+) Health | UNDERSTANDING LGBTQIA+ HEALTH DISPARITIES
items.title: High Rates of HIV and STIs in Men Who Have Sex with Men
items.content: Men who have sex with men face significantly higher rates of HIV and sexually transmitted infections. In 2020, they accounted for 68% of all new HIV diagnoses in the U.S., and in 2021, they represented 47% of primary and secondary syphilis infections.
keyPoints: Rates of human papillomavirus-associated anal cancers are 17 times higher among men who have sex with men compared to heterosexual men.
68% of all new HIV diagnoses in the U.S. were among gay and bisexual men in 2020.
47% of primary and secondary syphilis infections in the U.S. involved men who have sex with men in 2021.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer or Questioning, Intersex, Asexual, and More (LGBTQIA+) Health | UNDERSTANDING LGBTQIA+ HEALTH DISPARITIES

items.subtopic: Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer or Questioning, Intersex, Asexual, and More (LGBTQIA+) Health | UNDERSTANDING LGBTQIA+ HEALTH DISPARITIES
items.title: Suicide Risk Among LGBTQIA+ Youth
items.content: LGBTQIA+ youth experience significantly higher rates of suicide attempts and ideation. In a 2021 survey, 42% of LGBTQIA+ youth considered attempting suicide in the past year, with over half of transgender and nonbinary youth seriously considering it.
keyPoints: 42% of LGBTQIA+ youth ages 13–24 seriously considered attempting suicide in the last year.
More than half of transgender and nonbinary youth considered suicide attempts.
LGB youth are four times more likely to attempt suicide compared to their heterosexual peers.
61% of gender-variant youth reported suicidal ideation at some point in their life.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, Queer or Questioning, Intersex, Asexual, and More (LGBTQIA+) Health | UNDERSTANDING LGBTQIA+ HEALTH DISPARITIES

items.subtopic: CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)
items.title: Distinguishing Gender Identity from Sexual Orientation
items.content: Understanding the difference between gender identity and sexual orientation is crucial for providing appropriate care to LGBTQIA+ patients. Gender identity refers to a person's internal sense of their gender, which may not align with their sex assigned at birth. Sexual orientation involves attraction, behavior, and identity dimensions.
keyPoints: Gender identity is an internal sense of one's gender beyond the binary male/female.
Sexual orientation includes attraction, behavior, and identity.
Common terms for sexual identity: gay, lesbian, bisexual, straight, heterosexual, homosexual, asexual.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)
items.title: Developing Comfort in Sexual Health Discussions
items.content: Clinicians should develop comfort discussing sexual health and intimacy to provide relevant care. Asking about sexual activity, partners, frequency, and types of interactions helps tailor discussions on STI risks and prevention strategies.
keyPoints: Ask if a patient is sexually active and details about their sexual practices.
Discuss HIV/STI risk factors based on sexual behavior (e.g., receptive anal sex without condoms).
Recommend screenings for STIs, PrEP, PEP, and condom use as appropriate.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)
items.title: Creating a Welcoming Health Care Environment
items.content: Hospitals and clinics should establish nondiscrimination policies, equal visitation rights, and staff training to create an inclusive environment for LGBTQIA+ patients. Collecting sexual orientation and gender identity information in electronic health records is essential for personalized care.
keyPoints: Establish nondiscrimination policies including gender identity and sexual orientation protections.
Implement equal visitation policies for LGBTQIA+ patients.
Collect SOGI data using three key questions to enhance personalized care.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 2)
items.title: Importance of Inclusive Physical Environments for LGBTQIA+ Patients
items.content: The physical environment in healthcare settings plays a crucial role in signaling safety and inclusivity to LGBTQIA+ patients. Most clinical spaces currently do not reflect this, often featuring heteronormative imagery. Incorporating LGBTQIA+-friendly images and text can help create a welcoming atmosphere.
keyPoints: Most health care materials feature heterosexual individuals or couples.
Adding LGBTQIA+-friendly images and text signals safety for sexual and gender minorities.
Identifying LGBTQIA+-competent providers through websites, buttons, and pins aids patient comfort.
Designating all-gender bathrooms is crucial for welcoming transgender and gender-nonconforming individuals.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 2) | FUTURE DIRECTION IN LGBT HEALTH

items.subtopic: FUTURE DIRECTION IN LGBT HEALTH
items.title: Addressing Health Disparities in the LGBTQIA+ Community
items.content: Despite improvements in social acceptance, many LGBTQIA+ individuals face discrimination and health disparities due to inequitable healthcare policies. Addressing these issues requires better data collection on LGBTQIA+ populations and understanding their unique health needs.
keyPoints: LGBTQIA+ individuals often experience discrimination, stigmatization, and violence.
Healthcare disparities arise from inequitable policies and limited awareness of LGBTQIA+ health issues.
Improved data collection and research on intersectionality are needed to address these barriers.
Focus on outcomes-focused research across the life course is essential for high-quality care.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 2) | FUTURE DIRECTION IN LGBT HEALTH

items.explanation: The passage states that 40% of transgender young adults and adults reported attempting suicide.

items.explanation: The passage mentions that gay and bisexual men accounted for 68% of all new HIV diagnoses in the United States.

items.question: What is the prevalence of substance abuse disorders among LGBTQIA+ adults compared to the general population?
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence of substance abuse disorders is 20–30% among LGBTQIA+ adults compared with ~9% in the general population.

items.question: What percentage of transgender individuals reported being subjected to harsh or abusive language by health care professionals according to a large U.S. survey?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that almost 21% of transgender people reported being subjected to harsh or abusive language by health care professionals.

items.subtopic: CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following best describes gender identity?
options: A person's internal sense of their gender, which may not align with sex assigned at birth.
The physical and biological characteristics determined at birth.
How one thinks about their attraction to others.
A term used exclusively for individuals who identify as transgender.
items.explanation: Gender identity is a person's internal sense of their gender, which may not align with sex assigned at birth.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which dimension of sexual orientation refers to one's desire to be with someone?
options: Attraction
Behavior
Identity
None of the above
items.explanation: Attraction is the dimension that refers to one's desire to be with someone.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 1)
items.question: What should clinicians inquire about when discussing sexual health and intimacy?
options: If a patient is sexually active, with whom, how often, and types of physical interactions.
Only the frequency of sexual activity.
The number of partners without regard to types of sex.
Whether the patient has had gender affirmation surgery.
items.explanation: Clinicians should inquire if a patient is sexually active, with whom, how often, and what types of physical interactions they have.

items.question: Which statement is true regarding transgender individuals and health screenings?
options: Many transgender people retain their natal sex organs, so appropriate cancer screening is important.
All transgender people have had gender affirmation surgery.
Transgender men do not need any specific health screenings.
Sexual orientation should be assumed for all transgender patients.
items.explanation: Many transgender individuals retain their natal sex organs, making appropriate cancer screening important.

items.subtopic: CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 2) | FUTURE DIRECTION IN LGBT HEALTH
items.question: Which of the following actions can help signal that a clinical space is safe for LGBTQIA+ patients?
options: Adding LGBTQIA+-friendly images and text to health care materials.
Using only traditional gender symbols in patient forms.
Limiting information about provider competencies online.
Designating single-gender bathrooms exclusively.
items.explanation: Incorporating LGBTQIA+-friendly images and text into healthcare materials helps signal inclusivity and safety for sexual and gender minorities.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 2) | FUTURE DIRECTION IN LGBT HEALTH

items.question: What is one way to help patients identify LGBTQIA+-competent providers?
options: Using websites, buttons, and pins.
Avoiding any mention of provider competencies.
Displaying only heterosexual couple images.
Limiting information about all-gender bathrooms.
items.explanation: Websites, buttons, and pins can be used to easily identify LGBTQIA+-competent providers, helping patients feel more comfortable.
items.reference: Lesbian Gay Bisexual Transgender Queer or Questioning Intersex Asexual · CREATING POSITIVE HEALTH EXPERIENCES FOR LGBTQIA+ PATIENTS (part 2) | FUTURE DIRECTION IN LGBT HEALTH

items.question: What is an important aspect of creating welcoming spaces for transgender and gender-nonconforming individuals?
options: Designating all-gender bathrooms.
Using only traditional gender symbols in signage.
Limiting LGBTQIA+-friendly images to private areas.
Avoiding any changes to physical environments.
items.explanation: Designating all-gender bathrooms is crucial for making clinical spaces welcoming to transgender and gender-nonconforming individuals.
